cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A type of learning where an organism associates two stimuli, learning to respond to the first as it would to the second

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A stimulus that naturally and automatically triggers a response without prior learning

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The unlearned, naturally occurring response to the unconditioned stimulus

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A previously neutral stimulus that, after association with the US, comes to trigger a conditioned response

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The learned response to the conditioned stimulus

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The initial learning stage in classical conditioning when the CS-US association is being established

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The diminishing of a conditioned response when the CS is repeatedly presented without the US

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The reappearance of an extinguished conditioned response after a rest period

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The tendency to respond to stimuli similar to the conditioned stimulus

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The learned ability to distinguish between the CS and other stimuli that do not signal the US

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: When a CS is paired with a new NS to produce a new CR, without the original US being present

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A type of learning in which behavior is strengthened or weakened by its consequences

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Adding a desirable stimulus after a behavior to increase its frequency

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Removing an aversive stimulus after a behavior to increase its frequency

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Adding an aversive stimulus after a behavior to decrease its frequency

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Removing a desirable stimulus after a behavior to decrease its frequency

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Reinforcing successive approximations toward a desired behavior until the full behavior is achieved

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A reinforcement schedule that delivers reinforcement after a fixed number of responses

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A reinforcement schedule that delivers reinforcement after an unpredictable number of responses, producing the highest response rate

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A reinforcement schedule that delivers reinforcement for the first response after a fixed time period

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A reinforcement schedule that delivers reinforcement for the first response after varying time intervals

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: An innately satisfying stimulus (like food or water) that requires no learning to be reinforcing

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A stimulus that gains reinforcing power through its association with a primary reinforcer (e.g., money, grades)

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A cue that signals whether a particular response will be reinforced

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Learning by watching and imitating others; also called social learning or modeling

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Learning that occurs without reinforcement and is not demonstrated until there is motivation to do so

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A sudden realization of a problem's solution without systematic trial and error

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A learned aversion to a specific food associated with illness, even when illness occurs hours after consumption

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The tendency for animals to revert to biologically predisposed behaviors that interfere with conditioned behaviors

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The hopelessness and passive resignation learned when an organism cannot avoid repeated aversive events

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A mental representation of a spatial layout or environment learned through experience

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: An operant conditioning system where desired behaviors earn tokens that can be exchanged for privileges or rewards

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Reinforcement that occurs every time the desired behavior is performed

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Reinforcement that occurs only some of the time when the desired behavior is performed, leading to more resistant-to-extinction behaviors

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The finding that partially reinforced behaviors are more resistant to extinction than continuously reinforced behaviors

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Ivan Pavlov
cards.answer: Discovered classical conditioning through his research on salivation in dogs; demonstrated that reflexive responses can be triggered by learned associations

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: B.F. Skinner
cards.answer: Pioneer of operant conditioning; developed the Skinner box to study reinforcement and punishment; emphasized environmental consequences in shaping behavior

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Albert Bandura
cards.answer: Demonstrated observational (social) learning through the Bobo doll experiment, showing children learn aggressive behavior by watching adult models

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Edward Thorndike
cards.answer: Formulated the law of effect, stating that responses followed by satisfying consequences are strengthened; used puzzle boxes with cats

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: John B. Watson
cards.answer: Founded behaviorism; conducted the Little Albert experiment demonstrating classical conditioning of fear in a human infant

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Edward Tolman
cards.answer: Demonstrated latent learning and cognitive maps in rats navigating mazes, showing learning can occur without reinforcement

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Wolfgang Köhler
cards.answer: Demonstrated insight learning in chimpanzees, showing they could solve problems through sudden mental reorganization rather than trial and error

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: John Garcia
cards.answer: Demonstrated biological preparedness in learning through taste aversion studies, showing organisms are predisposed to associate taste with illness

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Martin Seligman
cards.answer: Discovered learned helplessness in dogs, demonstrating that organisms exposed to unavoidable aversive events learn passive resignation

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Robert Rescorla
cards.answer: Demonstrated that classical conditioning depends on the predictive value (contingency) of the CS for the US, not just contiguity (pairing)

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Example of positive reinforcement
cards.answer: A teacher gives a student a sticker (adding a pleasant stimulus) for completing homework, increasing the likelihood the student will complete homework again
alternate_answers: Receiving a bonus for good sales

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Example of negative reinforcement
cards.answer: A person takes aspirin (removing a headache) and is more likely to take aspirin in the future when they have a headache
alternate_answers: Fastening seatbelt to stop annoying buzzer

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Example of positive punishment
cards.answer: A child touches a hot stove (adding pain) and is less likely to touch the stove in the future
alternate_answers: Receiving a scolding for bad behavior

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Example of negative punishment
cards.answer: A teenager loses phone privileges (removing a pleasant stimulus) for breaking curfew, decreasing the likelihood of breaking curfew again

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Example of a variable-ratio reinforcement schedule
cards.answer: Slot machines pay out after an unpredictable number of plays, producing high and steady response rates

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Example of stimulus generalization in classical conditioning
cards.answer: Little Albert, conditioned to fear a white rat, also showed fear of a rabbit, a dog, and a fur coat — similar stimuli to the original CS
alternate_answers: Fearing all dogs after being bitten by one
Little Albert fearing similar furry objects

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Thorndike's principle that behaviors followed by favorable consequences become more likely, and behaviors followed by unfavorable consequences become less likely

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The idea that organisms are biologically predisposed to learn certain associations more readily than others

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Bandura's concept that learning, cognition, behavior, and environment all interact to influence one another

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Example of shaping
cards.answer: Training a dog to roll over by first rewarding lying down, then turning to one side, then completing the full roll — reinforcing each step closer to the target behavior
alternate_answers: Training a pigeon to turn in a circle

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Example of fixed-interval schedule
cards.answer: Checking the mailbox increases as delivery time approaches each day, then drops off right after mail arrives — the 'scalloped' response pattern
alternate_answers: Studying increases before a scheduled exam

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The process in classical conditioning where the predictive relationship (contingency) between the CS and US determines whether conditioning occurs, not just temporal pairing

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Keller and Marian Breland
cards.answer: Demonstrated instinctive drift — trained animals reverting to species-specific behaviors that interfered with operantly conditioned responses

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Bandura's concept that one's belief in their ability to succeed in a specific situation influences whether they attempt and persist at a task

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A behavior modification technique pairing an unpleasant stimulus with an unwanted behavior to reduce that behavior (e.g., pairing alcohol with a nausea-inducing drug)

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A classical conditioning procedure where a CS is paired with a relaxation response to gradually reduce anxiety toward a feared stimulus

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Example of observational learning in everyday life
cards.answer: A child learns to tie their shoes by watching a parent demonstrate the steps, then imitating the behavior
